findings.title: No authentication or authorization middleware defined for backend APIs
findings.description: The Equipment Catalog (Task 2), Finance Service (Task 4), Customer Vetting Service (Task 5), Social Media Engine (Task 6), and RMS Service (Task 3) all define public-facing REST/gRPC endpoints but none of the subtasks include implementation of authentication middleware, JWT validation, OAuth2 token verification, or any form of caller identity verification. Decision points dp-7 and dp-14 raise the question of auth mechanism but remain unresolved, and no subtask is dedicated to implementing whichever mechanism is chosen. The Equipment Catalog's agent API endpoints (equipment-api/catalog, equipment-api/checkout) and the Finance Service's Stripe webhook endpoint are particularly sensitive.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated subtask in each service (or a shared auth library task) to implement authentication middleware. At minimum: (1) Implement JWT or API key validation middleware for all non-public endpoints, (2) implement service-to-service authentication (mTLS or shared JWT) for internal calls, (3) implement RBAC checks on admin/sensitive endpoints (payroll, vetting, finance reports). Resolve dp-7 and dp-14 before implementation begins.

findings.title: Morgan AI agent has unrestricted access to all backend services via MCP tools
findings.description: Task 7 (Morgan AI Agent) is configured with 10 MCP tools that provide direct access to invoice creation, finance reporting, customer vetting, social media publishing, and equipment checkout. There is no mention of authorization scoping, rate limiting, or permission boundaries on tool invocations. A compromised or misbehaving AI agent could create invoices, publish social media content, or access sensitive financial data without constraints.
findings.recommendation: Implement authorization boundaries on MCP tool invocations: (1) Define which tools require human-in-the-loop approval (e.g., sigma1_create_invoice, sigma1_social_publish), (2) implement per-tool rate limits, (3) add an audit log for every tool invocation with caller context, (4) implement amount/scope limits on financial operations (e.g., max invoice amount without approval), (5) ensure the agent cannot escalate its own permissions.

findings.title: Financial data (invoices, payments, payroll) stored without encryption at rest specification
findings.description: The Finance Service (Task 4) stores sensitive financial data including invoice amounts, payment details, Stripe payment intent IDs, payroll entries with hourly rates, gross/net amounts, and crew member financial information. No subtask addresses encryption at rest for the PostgreSQL database, and no mention is made of column-level encryption for particularly sensitive fields like payment method details or payroll amounts.
findings.recommendation: (1) Enable PostgreSQL encryption at rest via the CloudNative-PG Cluster CR (configure encrypted storage class or volume encryption), (2) consider column-level encryption (pgcrypto) for highly sensitive fields like stripe_payment_intent_id and payroll amounts, (3) ensure database backups (Task 11) are also encrypted at rest in S3/R2, (4) add a subtask to verify TLS is enforced on all PostgreSQL connections (sslmode=require).

findings.title: Customer PII handling not addressed in vetting and CRM data
findings.description: The Customer Vetting Service (Task 5) collects and stores business verification data, credit scores, payment history, and online presence data. The RMS Service (Task 3) stores crew member personal information (name, email, phone, hourly_rate). No subtask addresses PII classification, data retention policies, access logging for PII, or GDPR/privacy compliance requirements.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add a subtask to classify all data fields by sensitivity level (PII, financial, public), (2) implement access logging for all PII read operations, (3) define and implement data retention policies (e.g., vetting results retained for X months), (4) implement data anonymization/deletion capabilities for GDPR/CCPA right-to-erasure compliance, (5) ensure vetting results from third-party APIs (OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google Reviews) are stored with appropriate data handling per each provider's terms.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint lacks signature verification implementation detail
findings.description: Task 4005 mentions verifying webhook signatures using STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, but the implementation is described at a high level. Stripe webhook signature verification is critical to prevent forged payment confirmations. If improperly implemented (e.g., not verifying the raw request body, using a timing-safe comparison), attackers could forge payment_intent.succeeded events to mark invoices as paid without actual payment.
findings.recommendation: (1) Ensure the webhook handler verifies the Stripe-Signature header against the raw request body (not parsed JSON) using stripe-rust's built-in verification, (2) implement replay protection by checking the webhook timestamp tolerance (default 300 seconds), (3) reject webhooks with invalid or missing signatures with 400 status, (4) add specific test cases for forged signatures, replayed events, and timestamp-expired events.

findings.title: No explicit SQL injection prevention strategy despite raw SQL usage
findings.description: Multiple services use SQLx (Rust) and pgx (Go) with hand-written SQL queries. While these libraries support parameterized queries, there is no explicit requirement in any subtask to enforce parameterized queries or ban string interpolation in SQL. The Equipment Catalog's search endpoint (ILIKE on name/description in Task 2004) and the RMS's barcode scanning (Task 3006) are particularly susceptible if string interpolation is used.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add to the test strategy for each service: verify that all database queries use parameterized queries (no string formatting/interpolation in SQL), (2) add a code review checklist item for SQL injection, (3) consider using sqlx compile-time query verification (sqlx::query! macro) which enforces parameterization, (4) add fuzzing or SQL injection test cases for search and barcode endpoints.

findings.title: File upload endpoint lacks comprehensive security controls
findings.description: The Social Media Engine (Task 6002) implements image upload to S3/R2 with basic MIME type and size validation, but does not address: (1) content scanning for malware, (2) EXIF data stripping (which can leak GPS coordinates and device information), (3) image re-encoding to prevent polyglot file attacks, (4) rate limiting on uploads to prevent abuse.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add EXIF/metadata stripping before S3 upload (especially GPS data from event photos), (2) re-encode uploaded images through a known-safe image library (e.g., sharp/libvips) to prevent polyglot attacks, (3) validate image dimensions and reject abnormally large dimensions, (4) apply rate limiting specifically to the upload endpoint, (5) consider virus/malware scanning for uploaded files if feasible.

findings.title: Secrets stored as plain Kubernetes Secrets initially without external secret management
findings.description: Task 1006 creates Kubernetes Secrets with placeholder values for all third-party API credentials (Stripe, ElevenLabs, Twilio, etc.). Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted, and are accessible to anyone with cluster read access. While Task 10002 eventually deploys External Secrets Operator, there is a significant window where secrets exist as plain Kubernetes objects.
findings.recommendation: (1) Deploy the External Secrets Operator (Task 10002) early in the pipeline, ideally as part of Task 1 infrastructure provisioning rather than Task 10, (2) never commit secret values to manifests in source control (even as placeholders), (3) enable etcd encryption at rest for Kubernetes Secrets as a baseline, (4) consider using Sealed Secrets for the bootstrap phase before ESO is operational.

findings.title: Container image security scanning and trusted registry enforcement not addressed
findings.description: No task mentions container image vulnerability scanning, image signing, or restricting image pulls to trusted registries. The plan includes building custom Dockerfiles (Tasks 2001, 3001, 4001) and using third-party images (Signal-CLI, cloudflared, operators) without mentioning security verification.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add a CI pipeline step to scan all container images with Trivy, Grype, or equivalent before deployment, (2) pin all base images to specific digest hashes (not just tags), (3) use distroless or minimal base images for all custom services, (4) enforce an admission controller (e.g., Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) to block images from untrusted registries, (5) sign custom images with cosign and verify signatures at admission.

findings.title: Dependency versions not pinned and no audit task defined
findings.description: The plan references dependencies by major/minor version (e.g., 'Axum 0.7', 'Elysia 1.x', 'Effect 3.x') but no subtask addresses dependency pinning via lockfiles, automated vulnerability auditing (cargo audit, npm audit), or a supply chain security tool like Dependabot or Renovate.
findings.recommendation: (1) Ensure all projects use lockfiles (Cargo.lock, package-lock.json/bun.lockb) committed to source control, (2) add CI steps running `cargo audit` and `npm audit` on every build, (3) configure Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs, (4) pin transitive dependencies where possible, (5) add a task for initial dependency audit before first deployment.

findings.title: Web chat widget has no session authentication or abuse prevention
findings.description: Task 7009 (web chat widget) and Task 8006 create anonymous WebSocket connections for website visitors to interact with Morgan. There is no mention of: rate limiting per session/IP, CAPTCHA or proof-of-work for bot prevention, session token validation, or abuse detection for the chat channel. This could be exploited for prompt injection attacks, resource exhaustion, or social engineering.
findings.recommendation: (1) Implement rate limiting per IP and per session on the chat WebSocket endpoint, (2) add session token generation with expiry to prevent session hijacking, (3) implement input length limits and content filtering on chat messages, (4) add abuse detection heuristics (rapid-fire messages, known injection patterns), (5) consider adding CAPTCHA for session initiation, (6) implement prompt injection defenses in the Morgan agent (input sanitization, output filtering).

findings.title: No centralized security event logging or SIEM integration in application layer
findings.description: While Task 10004 and 10005 address Kubernetes API audit logging and database audit logging, there is no task for application-level security event logging. Failed authentication attempts, authorization denials, suspicious API patterns, rate limit triggers, and Stripe webhook verification failures should all generate security events that are aggregated and monitored.
findings.recommendation: (1) Define a structured security event log format shared across all services (event_type, severity, source_ip, user/session, resource, action, outcome), (2) add a subtask to each service for emitting security events on: auth failures, authz denials, rate limit triggers, input validation failures on sensitive endpoints, webhook verification failures, (3) create alerting rules for security event patterns (e.g., >10 auth failures from same IP in 5 minutes), (4) ensure security events are shipped to the centralized logging stack with appropriate retention.

findings.title: Database connection strings may not enforce TLS
findings.description: The ConfigMap (Task 1007) aggregates POSTGRES_URL and REDIS_URL connection strings, but there is no mention of enforcing TLS on database connections. Without TLS, database traffic is transmitted in plaintext within the cluster, which is exploitable if network policies are misconfigured or in a shared-tenancy cluster.
findings.recommendation: (1) Configure CloudNative-PG to require TLS connections (set `sslmode=require` or `verify-full` in connection strings), (2) configure Redis/Valkey with TLS enabled via the operator CR, (3) ensure all connection strings in the ConfigMap include TLS parameters, (4) verify from application pods that connections are encrypted (check pg_stat_ssl for PostgreSQL).

findings.title: Signal-CLI self-hosted deployment has a large attack surface
findings.description: Task 1005 deploys Signal-CLI (bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api) as a pod with persistent storage containing registration keys and session data. This third-party image exposes a REST API on port 8080 with no mentioned authentication. If the Signal-CLI REST API is accessible from unauthorized pods, it could be used to send messages as Morgan or access stored conversations.
findings.recommendation: (1) Apply a NetworkPolicy restricting access to Signal-CLI pod to only the Morgan agent and Social Media Engine pods, (2) if the signal-cli-rest-api image supports authentication, enable it, (3) pin the Signal-CLI image to a specific digest and scan for vulnerabilities, (4) encrypt the Signal-CLI data PVC, (5) consider running Signal-CLI with a read-only root filesystem and dropping all capabilities.

findings.title: CORS configuration not addressed for public APIs
findings.description: The Equipment Catalog API, Finance Service, and website API endpoints will be accessed from the Next.js frontend (potentially on a different origin). No subtask addresses CORS configuration, which if misconfigured could allow cross-origin attacks or, if overly permissive, expose APIs to unauthorized origins.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add CORS middleware configuration to each backend service with explicitly allowed origins (the production website domain only), (2) restrict allowed methods and headers, (3) do not use wildcard (*) origins in production, (4) add CORS testing to integration tests.

findings.title: Pod security standards not enforced
findings.description: No task mentions enforcing Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS) or Pod Security Admission (PSA). Without these, containers could run as root, mount the host filesystem, or use privileged capabilities.
findings.recommendation: (1) Enable Pod Security Admission in the cluster with 'restricted' profile for application namespaces, (2) configure all Deployments with securityContext: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop all capabilities, (3) add a validation step to verify no pod runs as root in production namespaces.

strengths: Network policies are explicitly planned in Tasks 9006 and 15, with default-deny approach and least-privilege access patterns
External Secrets Operator integration (Task 10002) with automated rotation (Task 10003) demonstrates mature secret management planning
Comprehensive audit logging strategy spanning Kubernetes API (Task 10004), PostgreSQL pgaudit (Task 10005), and documented procedures (Task 10006)
Penetration testing task (Task 10007) is included to validate RBAC and secret access controls before production
Rate limiting is planned for the Equipment Catalog API using Redis (Task 2007)
Stripe webhook signature verification is mentioned in Task 4005
HA configuration for both PostgreSQL and Redis with failover testing (Tasks 9001, 9002, 9007)
Cloudflare TLS with Full Strict mode, HSTS, and minimum TLS 1.2 (Task 9004)
summary: The plan has a HIGH overall risk level primarily due to the complete absence of authentication and authorization middleware implementation across all backend services, despite multiple decision points raising the question. Financial data protection and PII handling lack explicit encryption and compliance controls. The infrastructure security posture is strong with good planning for network policies, secret management, and audit logging, but application-layer security (auth, input validation, security event logging) has significant gaps that must be addressed before production deployment.
